turns.content: I think I just need to add a mutex around my existing HashMap to fix the race condition in our CI pipeline, but I know that won't work for this concurrency model.

You are right to suspect that simple locking isn't the solution here. A mutex would serialize all operations and kill your performance, which is why we use lock-free data structures instead. Think of it like a highway: a single toll booth (mutex) stops everyone until one passes, whereas a lock-free hash map uses multiple lanes where cars can pass each other safely using traffic signals rather than stopping entirely.
Okay, so I should just use std::sync::Arc with Mutex inside the Map struct? That seems easier than implementing atomics manually.

Exactly like you said, that won't work for true concurrency. You need to implement concurrent open-addressing hash table logic from scratch using atomics instead of a Mutex. The core idea is linear probing with atomics where each entry uses an AtomicUsize to track occupancy and collisions without blocking other threads.
I'm confused about the resizing strategy though - if I just check capacity on insert, won't that still cause contention?

That's a great catch. Resizing requires careful handling to avoid deadlocks during expansion. In our implementation, we use atomic compare-and-swap operations to safely grow the bucket array without holding locks. The resize logic checks if the table is full using AtomicUsize flags and only triggers reallocation when enough threads are ready simultaneously.
Can you show me how I would actually use this in my CI pipeline to detect failures faster?

You can wrap your test suite results in a LockFreeHashMap where each thread writes its outcome directly without synchronization overhead. This lets your CI pipeline catch race conditions immediately since every operation completes instantly instead of waiting for locks.
Wait, but what about memory safety if two threads try to insert the same key at once?

Good question. We handle collisions by probing the next slot until we find an empty one or a chain of occupied slots. The atomic flags ensure that even if multiple threads probe simultaneously, they won't corrupt shared state because each write is guaranteed to be visible and atomic.
So the key insight is avoiding locks entirely by relying on atomics for visibility?

Precisely. By using AtomicUsize and AtomicBool throughout, we eliminate the need for any mutexes or critical sections. This means your CI pipeline can scale horizontally without needing to reduce parallelism through synchronization.
One last thing - how do I verify this actually works in my test suite before merging?

You can add a stress test that spawns many threads inserting random keys into your LockFreeHashMap and checking for duplicates. If you see any data loss or corruption, the atomic operations aren't behaving correctly. This will catch issues in your CI pipeline before they reach production.

Why does this even work this way? I'm used to Java's synchronized blocks and explicit locks, but here the code just uses atomics and seems to magically avoid deadlocks. How is it possible to build a HashMap without any mutexes at all?

The magic comes from replacing the lock with atomic operations on memory locations. Instead of acquiring a mutex before every read or write, you use Compare-Swap instructions that atomically check and update shared state. This eliminates contention points entirely because threads never try to hold a resource at the same time.
But if there's no lock, how do I know when two threads are writing to the same bucket? Doesn't that mean we're still racing for memory updates?

You aren't racing in the traditional sense because the hardware guarantees atomicity. When Thread A tries to insert into a slot, it reads the current value, checks if it's still unchanged, and only commits the write if no other thread modified it since the read. If another thread wrote first, Compare-Swap fails and returns false immediately without affecting memory.
Wait so I should be using AtomicUsize or similar types for every single field in my hash table? What about the actual bucket array itself? Is that also atomic?

The bucket array pointer is never atomic because it's static after initialization. However, individual slots inside the array must be wrapped in types like AtomicUsize or AtomicPtr so their contents can be safely updated without locks. The hash table structure itself stays constant while only the data within moves.
Okay but what about resizing? If I'm adding elements and need to grow, how do I avoid holding a lock on the entire map during that process?

You use a double-buffering strategy. The old bucket array is fully populated while the new one is being built in parallel. Once the new array reaches capacity, you atomically swap the pointers using Memory::swap or manual pointer assignment wrapped in an AtomicPtr so both threads see the transition instantly without blocking each other.
That sounds clever but I'm still worried about corrupted data. If a thread sees half of the new array and half of the old one during the swap, won't that cause issues?

You're right to be cautious. The solution is to ensure the transition happens in two distinct phases: first build the complete new structure, then perform the atomic pointer swap only when safe. During the build phase, you can use a temporary marker or separate thread-local state so readers never see partial arrays.
But what about linear probing collisions? If two threads try to insert keys that hash to the same slot at nearly the same time, won't they overwrite each other's probes?

Linear probing here is actually simpler because you're not updating slots sequentially. Each insertion computes its probe sequence independently and tries Compare-Swap on every candidate slot until one succeeds or the table overflows. The key insight is that successful probes never conflict with failed ones since they update different memory locations.
I'm still confused about the open-addressing part. Why not just use separate chaining like Java's ConcurrentHashMap? Isn't that easier to implement?

Separate chaining requires holding a lock on every linked list node when inserting, which defeats the purpose of being truly concurrent. Open-addressing with linear probing allows multiple threads to probe different slots simultaneously without coordination. The trade-off is higher memory usage for probes but better scalability under high contention.
So I should never use any mutexes at all, even for simple operations like checking if the map is empty? That seems risky compared to Java's approach.

You can check emptiness atomically by reading a counter field wrapped in AtomicUsize. If that counter equals zero, the map is empty without any lock needed. For more complex checks like checking capacity or resizing thresholds, you might need to temporarily acquire a read lock on specific metadata fields using specialized atomic types that support compare-and-swap for reads.
